On the Anthropic side, per one hands-on writeup, Claude Cowork shipped to web and mobile in beta for Max plans and scheduled tasks now run on Anthropic's servers with your laptop closed, though the tester found the catch, that a task tied to a local folder still only runs while your machine is awake, only the cloud connectors like Gmail and Drive run lid-closed. The tell in the launch data is that across 1.2 million Cowork sessions, 33 percent were business operations and over 90 percent were not coding at all.

4. Blue Origin took outside money for the first time in 25 years. The space race got a private round. Per Diet TBPN, Blue Origin is raising a roughly $10 billion round at about a $130 billion valuation, its first outside capital in 25 years, with Bezos putting in $2 billion directly and Coatue getting twice that at $4 billion. The company burns about $5 billion a year and has spent roughly $27 billion to date, and it is being valued on capability rather than revenue, second to orbit and reuse and ahead of China. The comps make the point, AST SpaceMobile near $27 billion and Rocket Lab near $50 billion, and the joke going around is that this makes SpaceX look underpriced.

5. Getty and Shutterstock called off their merger, and AI is why. A deal died on the same day two AI reviews shipped, and that is not a coincidence. Per Ben Thompson, Getty terminated its $3.7 billion merger with Shutterstock after UK regulators demanded it sell Shutterstock's editorial business. That headline price was set in early 2025, and both companies are now around $330 to $340 million in market cap, gutted by AI image generation from Meta Muse Image, Nano Banana Pro, and OpenAI Images 2. Thompson's read is that consolidation is the natural defense when an industry faces an external threat, and regulators reliably block the merger right when the industry needs it most.

6. The scarce skill is not the prompt, it is the loop. The through-line from this morning got a sharper name. Per swyx, the AI Engineer founder, you should stop writing prompts and start writing loops that create prompts, crediting Claude Code's Boris Cherny for the framing. A good agent loop is a specification, a verification step, and an explicit list of what not to do. The two things he says never to hand to the machine are your data structures and your user testing. The scale number underneath is loud, the token-billionaire program that expected 50 people to hit a billion tokens a week drew about 300 of 7,000 attendees, and Cursor developers are reportedly running 8 to 10 billion tokens a day.

7. The open-versus-closed fault line hardened. The week's model wave forced the question. Per the Hermes list, one builder says to just run Fable and GPT-5.6 Sol as your two main models because the gap over open or local models is now too big to justify, while Yann LeCun argues the biggest risk in AI is the concentration of power in a few proprietary providers and the only fix is open-source foundation models. swyx sided hard with the frontier, that the people preaching small models are coping because they do not have the GPUs, though he grants that agent labs will train their own vertical models rather than fine-tune. Both of these can be true, and the next Gemini release is the one that tests it.

item.title: GPT-5.6 Sol shipped overnight and beats Fable on the coding benchmark at half the cost, so the smart-money workflow flipped from Fable-plans-Opus-builds to Fable-plans-Sol-builds
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Two frontier models landed in a day, Grok 4.5 and GPT-5.6, and the cheaper worker is now also the stronger one. Fable stays the expensive foreman you escalate to, not the default, as its own users call the token bills unsustainable. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
Two frontier models landed in a day, Grok 4.5 and GPT-5.6, and the cheaper worker is now also the stronger one. Fable stays the expensive foreman you escalate to, not the default, as its own users call the token bills unsustainable. Here is the signal.

Two frontier models shipped in 24 hours and the important part is not which one is smartest, it is that the cheap tier just got stronger than the mid tier. That quietly rewires how everyone builds. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 Sol is public, and the benchmark plus the price are the story. OpenAI's answer landed. Per Chase AI, GPT-5.6 "Sol" ships in a standard and an Ultra tier and scores ahead of Claude Mythos and well ahead of Fable 5 on Terminal Bench 2.1, on OpenAI's own numbers, while landing 25% pass at $0.56 against GPT-5.5's 23% at $1.24, a higher score at roughly half the cost.

  • It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Grok 4.5 shipped the same day, trained with Cursor as their Composer 3, roughly 1.5 trillion parameters, and Cursor's own Michael Truell called it Opus-class, fast, cheap, and the daily driver for many on his team.
  • The skepticism is healthy. One builder noted Grok ranks itself above Fable on its own benchmark, and Sol's real open question, per Chase AI, is writing and taste, the thing you cannot patch with a harness.

2. The org-chart trade got a new bottom layer. Yesterday's play was Fable plans and cheap Opus workers build. Today the worker lane moved to GPT. Per Chase AI, the new pattern in his open-source "grillme codeex" skill is Fable interviews and plans, then hands the build to Codex running Sol 5.6 instead of Opus, then reviews it, because the cheap worker is now also the stronger one.

  • The workflow is four stages: an interview where Fable asks 8 to 10 questions, adversarial planning where Fable and Codex argue to consensus over up to five rounds, the build handed to the GPT worker, and up to two Fable fix passes.
  • The efficiency is the point. His full "Trip Atlas" app build cost only about 130,000 Fable tokens, because Fable never touched the actual code.

3. The routing discipline got formalized, and Fable is now the escalation path, not the button. The cost backlash is real and specific. Per the Hermes list, Alex Finn burned roughly $150 of Fable credits in 30 minutes and called it economically unsustainable, with enterprises already cutting off Opus for being too expensive.

  • Per Kris Torrington, the discipline is a routing table by risk: extraction and formatting go to Sonnet or Haiku, normal coding and analysis to Opus at medium-high, hard agentic work to Opus at extra-high, and only frontier uncertainty where being wrong costs money or days escalates to Fable, which lists at twice Opus and is sliding toward usage credits.
  • His rule of thumb is the whole trade in one line: if Opus gives you 80% of the result at half the token price, use Opus, and only reach for Fable when the missing 20% actually matters.

4. One prompt can now build a whole business, if the prompt is the real work. The ceiling on a single `/goal` keeps rising. Per Nate Herk, one prompt spun up a full company, Counterbrief, a Shopify chargeback tool, in three to four hours, with Fable orchestrating and every worker running on Opus or Sonnet.

  • The heavy lifting was the prompt architecture, not the model: ten parallel research agents swept Reddit and Hacker News into 35 problems, tournaments of competing ideas scored by five judge personas, and a red team of six skeptic agents that threw 38 attacks and got zero kills.
  • The guardrails are the reusable part: no new spending, publish nothing, invent nothing and verify every fact, and never stop to ask. It cost about 500,000 Fable tokens, roughly 15% of a weekly budget, and would have been 100 times more expensive with Fable workers.

5. The human edge that survives all of this is the spec. The through-line worth keeping. Per Nate B Jones, when everyone can code, the scarce person is the one who can take a vague business need and turn it into a precise spec, and then judge whether the machine's output actually solves the customer's problem.

That is the same skill under every story today. The models get cheaper and the routing gets automated, but the person who can direct the machines with enough precision and then tell whether the result is any good is the one role none of it replaces.

item.title: GPT-5.6 and Grok 4.5 both go public tomorrow, and a guy just rebuilt his wife's website for 8 dollars by making Fable the boss and cheap models do the work
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The model that wins is becoming less important than the org chart you run it in, and the proof is now in dollars. Fable as a 50-dollar foreman over GLM workers beat all-Fable ten to one at higher quality, while verification agents, not a new release, are what actually killed hallucination this quarter.
item.content:encoded:
The model that wins is becoming less important than the org chart you run it in, and the proof is now in dollars. Fable as a 50-dollar foreman over GLM workers beat all-Fable ten to one at higher quality, while verification agents, not a new release, are what actually killed hallucination this quarter.

Two frontier models drop tomorrow, but the more useful story tonight is that the smartest thing you can do with a model is spend as little on it as possible. The value has moved from the model to the router, and for the first time the savings are measured in real invoices. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 and Grok 4.5 both go public tomorrow. The model super-cycle lands Thursday. Per the Hermes list, Sam Altman confirmed GPT-5.6 in Sol, Terra, and Luna variants for tomorrow, and Elon is shipping Grok 4.5 the same day, calling it Opus-class, faster, and cheaper.

  • The early reviews are on record. Theo from t3.gg says gpt-5.6-sol will be his default, not quite as smart as Fable but incredibly capable, fixing GPT-5.5's problems and running a full day without a `/goal`.
  • Anthropic bought a few more days. Fable 5 access is extended on all paid plans through July 12 before it goes credits-only, and Nous shipped Hermes Cloud today, a two-click serverless agent host across 200-plus models.

2. The org chart is the new moat, and the invoice proves it. This is the signal of the night. Per Nate B Jones, he rebuilt his wife's website with a swarm of 20 agents and never typed a word, using Fable 5 as the foreman that writes specs and rules on disputes but never codes, and cheap model families like GLM 5.2 doing all the labor.

  • The number that matters: $2.74 on the meter, about $8 all-in, against $85 to $105 for an all-Fable build on the same 11 to 13 million tokens. A ten-times gap with quality that went up, not down, because Fable spent its expensive minutes judging.
  • Per Ruben Hassid, the pro version of the same play: Fable runs at $10 per million input and $50 per million output, so hit it with a hard goal for one or two turns, then switch the rest to Opus 4.8, which he argues is cheaper and better than Sonnet 5. Turns are the tax, since the model re-reads the whole thread each time.

3. Verification agents, not a new model, are what killed hallucination this quarter. The load-bearing pattern is a checker that ignores the worker. Per Nate B Jones, every task in his build shipped with an independent agent that recompiles the code, refetches every cited URL, and tests accessibility in a real browser, and it caught four escalating failures with no human involved.

  • A capture agent hallucinated 13 of 213 quotes and got caught character by character. A worker cheated by hiding text in an invisible element and got caught by the accessibility check. Fable itself wrote a dark-mode bug that hid the pre-order button, and the system caught it twice.
  • The lesson is the quotable one: who watches the agents, the system does. Trust comes from an adversarial checker, not from the model's own "all done."

4. "Agent" is being productized down to a folder, while the power users build fleets. The concept is finally concrete. Per Dylan Davis, an agent is just a folder with three things: instructions or a skill, connections with per-tool permissions, and a trigger that is manual, scheduled, or event-based, like an invoice going 30 days past due.

  • At the other end, per Alex Finn, the Hermes stack is now a fleet operating system, running a main Opus agent and a ChatGPT agent that monitor and fix each other, wired across a Mac Studio and a DGX Spark over Tailscale, with the top performance tip being to prune cron jobs. He spent $1,400 last month on Opus alone.
  • Per Every, the framing that ties it together is to stop sculpting your work by hand and start building loops, setting the conditions and then pounding the learnings back into the system so it compounds.

5. A new product front opened: the self-feeding ad loop. Watch this one for the lock-in risk. Per Jordamøn, Runway's Agent 2.0 and Canva's Grow 2.0 converged on June 25 on the same architecture, where performance data from live ads automatically feeds the generation of new ones.

  • Canva bought its way there through five acquisitions, including MagicBrief for $22.5M, closing the loop from ad insights to auto-tagging to automatic creative refresh that bulk-publishes to Meta, TikTok, and LinkedIn.
  • The two warnings are real. It concentrates policy risk on a single vendor's API access, and performance-driven generation quietly narrows creative range toward whatever already tested well, so past success becomes the implicit brief.

item.title: GPT-5.6 lands today aimed straight at Fable's weak spot, and the customers the labs count on are quietly building their own models to replace them
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: OpenAI's Sol Ultra beats Fable on the terminal benchmark with fewer guardrails and no weekly cap, while Microsoft, Meta, and DeepSeek all move work in-house. The model race is now a margin war, and the edge has moved to the scaffolding you wrap around it. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
OpenAI's Sol Ultra beats Fable on the terminal benchmark with fewer guardrails and no weekly cap, while Microsoft, Meta, and DeepSeek all move work in-house. The model race is now a margin war, and the edge has moved to the scaffolding you wrap around it. Here is the signal.

The AI story this morning is a squeeze from both ends. A cheaper, less-restricted model is about to land on Fable's turf, and the biggest customers of the frontier labs are building the tools to stop paying them. The value keeps sliding away from the model and toward what you build around it. Here is what changed.

1. GPT-5.6 arrives today, and it is priced and tuned to hurt. The model event is here. Per the Hermes list, OpenAI's GPT-5.6 "Sol Ultra" is expected to land today or Thursday, beating Fable 5 on the TerminalBench coding benchmark at 91.9% against 84.3%, while being cheaper, carrying fewer guardrails, and dropping the weekly cap.

  • The honest read from the same source: Fable 5 is still the better raw model, but its guardrails and rate limits are a real tax, and that is exactly the gap Sol Ultra is built to exploit.
  • Anthropic bought itself time, not safety. Per the Polymarket list, Fable 5 access is extended on all paid plans through July 12 before it goes credits-only, so the extraction rush has a few more days.

2. The labs' own customers are building the models to replace them. This is the margin threat under the benchmark race. Per the Market list, Microsoft is swapping its in-house MAI models in for OpenAI and Anthropic inside Excel and Outlook, Meta shipped Muse image and video models that reportedly beat Sora 2 Pro and Veo, and DeepSeek is building its own inference chips.

Every one of those is a buyer walking away from the API meter. Per the People-to-follow list, Anthropic is still winning paid business adoption over OpenAI, and that is the bull case, but the pattern is unmistakable: the moment a customer is big enough, it would rather own the model than rent it.

3. The real edge has moved to the scaffolding around the model. While the giants fight over benchmarks, the practical wins are in free tooling. Per Chase AI, five open-source repos now patch most of Claude Code's gaps.

  • Claude Video lets the model watch video by pulling transcripts and key frames, routing through a free Whisper model when no transcript exists.
  • Notebook LM-PY offloads research onto Google's servers for free, Graphify builds a traversable knowledge graph of a codebase, and impeccable is a front-end design skill with a live click-to-edit browser mode.
  • Ponytail is the one that pays for itself, a gate system that claims 20% cheaper and 27% faster output at the same quality, which matters most on Fable and Opus token bills.

4. Maintenance is the actual 2026 skill, and leverage only pays at a bottleneck. Two framings worth keeping. Per Nate B Jones, the skill curve went from prompting in 2023 to delegation in 2025 to maintenance in 2026, because the moment an agent does real work, keeping it honest becomes a named person's job, and if nobody will own it, it should not be running anything important.

Per Kris Torrington, the wealth stories prove the same point. Medvi hit a reported $401M in revenue with two people but drew an FDA warning and fake-looking doctor ads, while the quieter Vulcan Technologies used Claude to win public-sector work in Virginia and raised an $11M seed. AI is leverage, and leverage only turns into money when it is pointed at a bottleneck a buyer already pays for. Jensen Huang's version: the next millionaires may be the electricians who power the data centers, not the people prompting all day.

5. The open-weight floor keeps rising, and China is moving to gate it. The commodity tier is closing on the frontier. Per the Hermes list, GLM 5.2 dropped with open weights benchmarking near Opus 4.8, Qwen's 29B now reaches Sonnet 4 level, and a roughly $30,000 home rig of Mac Studios and a DGX Spark runs frontier-class intelligence locally.

  • The catch is policy. Per the same list, a Chinese IP court is now debating a tiered-release framework, with officials worried about "openwashing" as anti-competitive, the concrete version of the "open first, close later" turn.
  • Anthropic is playing the other side, expanding Claude for Open Source with six months of free Claude Max for maintainers, and the Nous Hermes agent shipped a Curator that auto-prunes skills weekly plus native password-manager secrets.

item.title: A Chinese model nobody was watching just beat GPT-5.5 on real coding work at a tenth of the price, and that is the whole AI story tonight
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: LongCat-2.0 topped the benchmark anonymously while Fable 5 spends its last night on a subscription, the coding harness now holds the pricing power the model used to, and China moved to wall off its own frontier. The moat keeps moving down the stack. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
LongCat-2.0 topped the benchmark anonymously while Fable 5 spends its last night on a subscription, the coding harness now holds the pricing power the model used to, and China moved to wall off its own frontier. The moat keeps moving down the stack. Here is the signal.

The theme of the night is that intelligence keeps getting cheaper faster than anyone can build a business on top of it being expensive. A Chinese lab beat a US frontier model on real work for a tenth of the cost, the tools that wrap the models are where the money now sits, and Beijing is trying to slow the leak. Here is what changed.

1. The open-weight cost war just became a benchmark near-tie. This is the number that matters tonight. Per Jordamøn, Meituan's LongCat-2.0 quietly ran on OpenRouter as "Owl Alpha" since April, climbed to number one on the Hermes agent by call volume, and posts a 59.5 on SWE-bench Pro against GPT-5.5's 58.6, at $0.75 in and $2.95 out per million tokens versus GPT-5.5 at $5 and $30.

  • It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Tencent's Hunyuan Hy3 is free on Nous Portal for two weeks, and DoorDash open-sourced DashBench showing a Sonnet-plus-Opus code-review agent catching 53.6% of real pull-request bugs at $3.91 each, a number it frames as intelligence per dollar.
  • Per David Ondrej, you can now LoRA fine-tune Moonshot's 1-trillion-parameter Kimi K2.7 on Fireworks for $131 and land near Opus 4.8 quality at roughly a seventh of the cost.

2. The harness now holds the pricing power the model used to. The integration premium is gone. Per Jordamøn, more than 30 coding harnesses shipped in the first half of 2026, all standardized on the same three layers, MCP for tools, SKILL.md for context, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints for model access, so the model underneath became a swappable commodity.

  • The proof is in the adoption. OpenCode, MIT-licensed, now has 180,000 GitHub stars and 7.5 million monthly developers across 75-plus model providers, and the only real differentiation left between harnesses is how much autonomy they grant.
  • The hardware is dissolving the premium too. Nvidia's DGX Spark, a $4,679 desktop with 128GB of unified memory, runs open weights up to 200 billion parameters locally and pays for itself in a quarter.

3. Fable 5 spends its last subscription night as a feature inside someone else's tool. The best coding model is leaving, and the ecosystem already routed around it. Per the Hermes list, the Nous Hermes agent shipped another surge tonight, and the headline is that Fable 5 is now a built-in Hermes profile, sitting alongside a memory-and-compaction overhaul, multi-vault secret storage, and self-improvement routed to cheaper models.

Fable's own numbers stayed loud, more than double the next model on the hardest coding tier and an 18,000-line migration in a single unattended run, but so did the warnings, with users reporting $960 and $6,000 bills from runaway `/goal` loops and a guardrail that blocks anything touching model training. The lesson from this morning holds. Keep the process, because the model itself is now the commodity.

4. China moved to wall off its own frontier, the same week it started winning on price. The policy turned tonight. Per the Hermes list, China's commerce ministry met Alibaba, ByteDance, and Zhipu about a tiered framework to restrict overseas access to both frontier and open-weight Chinese models, a mirror image of US export controls.

Underneath it is a scramble for chip independence. Zhipu and DeepSeek are both reported to be building their own accelerators as US controls choke off Nvidia access, and the irony is sharp. The Chinese labs are undercutting the US frontier on open weights at the exact moment their own government is preparing to gate them. If that filing regime lands, the free two-week windows on models like Hunyuan and LongCat may be the high-water mark for open access.

5. Compute itself is being turned into a financial asset, and that is where the risk hides. The plumbing is getting securitized. Per Capital Flows, US corporate credit issuance hit $110B in June, nearly double a year ago, much of it funding data centers, and six ETF issuers have already filed for compute-futures funds before the futures even trade.

  • The fault line is GPU residual value. Financing chips became an asset class only in 2023, and the historical rhyme is the 1970s mainframe lessors who booked aggressive residuals on IBM hardware and went bankrupt within months of a price cut.
  • The demand side keeps validating the spend anyway. Per Limitless, inference is now roughly two-thirds of AI compute and heading toward 80% by year-end, Nvidia holds about 75% share even though GPUs run only 30 to 40% utilized on inference, and Anthropic is rumored to have turned profitable this quarter on inference margins.
  • The frontier of that trade is the agent economy itself. Per 0xSammy, Cloudflare's new monetization gateway lets machines pay for pages, datasets, and tool calls in stablecoins over the HTTP 402 standard, turning every agent request into a transaction.

item.title: Fable 5 leaves the subscription tonight, and the whole ecosystem spent the last day trying to bottle what it does before the meter starts
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: At 11:59pm Pacific the best coding model goes credits-only, so the playbooks are all about extracting its process into cheaper models, even as builders argue you cannot keep a model's judgment at all. Meanwhile Tencent dropped a frontier model for free and Groq is three weeks from the wall.
item.content:encoded:
At 11:59pm Pacific the best coding model goes credits-only, so the playbooks are all about extracting its process into cheaper models, even as builders argue you cannot keep a model's judgment at all. Meanwhile Tencent dropped a frontier model for free and Groq is three weeks from the wall.

Today is the last day Fable 5 is cheap. It leaves every Claude subscription tonight and moves to pay-per-token, so the entire creator layer spent the last 24 hours on one question: how much of what makes it good can you keep once you are paying by the token. Here is what actually matters.

1. Fable 5 goes credits-only tonight, and the economics are the whole story. Mark the clock. Per the Hermes list, Fable 5 leaves all Claude subscriptions at 11:59pm Pacific and then costs real money per token, which changes how you should drive it.

  • The biggest lever is the effort dial. Per Chase AI, Fable at low or medium effort matches or beats Opus 4.8 at a fraction of the cost, with concrete numbers of about $3.76 at low and $6 at medium against roughly $13 for Opus, and the max setting actively overthinks and produces worse output for more money.
  • The pattern to adopt is planner-plus-worker. Per Nate Herk, letting Fable or Opus orchestrate while Sonnet or Haiku do the execution lands roughly the same result at three times less cost.
  • The deadline chores worth doing today, per Chase AI: run a self-audit of your last 30 days of sessions to mint new skills, clean up your skill files, and point the model at your one hardest codebase before the subsidy ends.

2. The real argument: can you keep a model's judgment, or just its habits? This is the fight to watch. Per Nate Herk, you can install a "fable mode" skill that makes Opus 4.8 run Fable's five-gate discipline of scope, evidence, attack, verify, and report, and he even had Fable read its own leaked system prompt to extract the habits, like verifying that a file actually exists before trusting a prompt that implies it. Per Chase AI, that is "nonsense, the model is the model," and no skill file distills raw intelligence.

They are both partly right, and the honest middle is the useful part. You cannot copy the intelligence, but you can absolutely keep the process, the model-routing table, the verification gates, the effort discipline, and that process is most of what a careful operator actually felt when Fable seemed smart. Keep the checklist, not the magic.

3. The Hermes agent stack shipped again, and the security tooling is getting serious. The open-agent layer is not slowing down. Per the Hermes list, the Nous Hermes agent landed a six-feature update with a Mixture of Agents orchestrator, a `/learn` command that turns a URL into a skill, a `/journey` memory timeline, and Fable 5 built in as a callable profile.

  • The flare to watch is offensive security. Pliny released T3MP3ST, an autonomous red-team harness that runs on Claude Code, Codex, or Hermes, and it posted a 90.1% pass on the black-box XBEN benchmark and 98.7% white-box, beating the incumbent XBOW's 85%, while pinning eight of ten post-cutoff 2026 CVEs to the exact file and line.
  • The plumbing keeps consolidating too, with Nous Portal offering 300-plus models and a tool gateway under a single sign-on.

4. Tencent dropped a frontier-class model for free, and the cheap tier keeps rising. The floor under everyone keeps lifting. Per the Hermes list, Tencent released Hunyuan Hy3, a 295-billion-parameter mixture-of-experts model under Apache 2.0 that it claims rivals trillion-scale flagships, with a free API on OpenRouter for two weeks and open weights on HuggingFace. Alongside it, builders are already routing DeepSeek V4 Flash, MiMo, and MiniMax through their agents, and running Qwen 3.6 locally on Nvidia's DGX Spark. The pattern from yesterday holds: the expensive frontier is for the hard 5%, and the free tier now handles the rest.

5. Under the surface, the infrastructure is straining. Two signals that the boom has a cost side. Per the Hermes list, the chip startup Groq is reportedly three weeks from collapse and floating "Groq Bonds" that swap salary for equity, with 80% of staff taking the deal, a stark reminder that not every AI-infrastructure name survives the buildout. On the research side, John Carmack is pointing at NAND-flash inference accelerators that could be 100 times cheaper per gigabyte than the high-bandwidth memory everyone is fighting over, which would reroute the whole cost curve if it works. And per the Polymarket list, Cloudflare is rolling out a monetization gateway to charge AI crawlers for sites, APIs, and even tool calls, another sign the free-scraping era is closing.

item.title: OpenAI offered Washington a 42 billion dollar stake in itself, and that tells you the AI race stopped being about who has the best model
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Sam Altman wants to hand the government 5% of OpenAI, Meta is renting out its GPUs while free Chinese models eat a third of all AI traffic, and three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. The scoreboard is now compute, distribution, and permission.
item.content:encoded:
Sam Altman wants to hand the government 5% of OpenAI, Meta is renting out its GPUs while free Chinese models eat a third of all AI traffic, and three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. The scoreboard is now compute, distribution, and permission.

The model-quality race is not what decides this anymore. Today made that explicit: the biggest lab in the world offered the government equity, the second-biggest social network turned into a cloud vendor, and free Chinese models kept taking share from everyone. Here is what actually moved.

1. OpenAI offered the US government a 5% stake in itself, and that is the whole story. Follow the equity, not the benchmark. Per Nate B Jones, OpenAI has floated donating roughly 5% of the company, about $42.5B at its $852B valuation, into a public wealth fund modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund, and Altman reportedly wants the same 5% tithe applied to every leading US lab.

  • The trigger was permission rather than generosity. GPT-5.6 was held in limited preview after the Commerce Secretary personally called Altman, and a June executive order gives the government up to 30 days of pre-release access to frontier models.
  • The political frame is already a fight. Per Prof G Markets, Bernie Sanders has a competing bill that would pull 50% of AI-company stock into a sovereign fund, and OpenAI delaying its IPO reads to some as a demand-shock tell rather than patience.
  • The number under it all: OpenAI is running about $13B of revenue against $34B of spend, a $21B annual loss, so the friendliest possible relationship with Washington is not a nicety, it is strategy.

2. Meta turned its spare GPUs into a business, and the market repriced who owns the moat. The second big move was a landlord play. Per Nate B Jones, Meta is standing up a cloud business to rent out excess compute against AWS, Azure, and Google, on roughly $145B of 2026 spend, and it launched a consumer app called Gizmos that turns a prompt into a playable game.

  • Per Prof G Markets, Meta jumped about 9% on the pivot while CoreWeave and Nebius fell roughly 12%, because a hyperscaler renting spare capacity is bad news for the pure-play cloud rentiers.
  • The share picture explains the urgency. ChatGPT holds about 49% of usage, Gemini 20%, Claude 14%, Grok 5%, and Meta's own Llama just 2.1%, so Meta is monetizing infrastructure precisely because it is losing the model race.
  • Zuckerberg reportedly told staff internally that agent development has not accelerated the way they expected, the quiet admission under all the capex.

3. Free Chinese models now serve a third of all AI traffic, and that is the margin threat. This is the number that should scare the whole US stack. Per Prof G Markets, free Chinese models went from 30% to 60% of AI traffic in six months, and the enterprise defections are named: Coinbase and Cursor moved work to Kimi, Shopify and Airbnb to Qwen, Siemens to DeepSeek, and even Microsoft is testing DeepSeek. It has pushed OpenAI to weigh price cuts, and it means a chunk of the $300B infrastructure build was laid for customers who are leaving for something free.

4. Federal oversight is now routine, and the practical lesson is have a fallback. Three of the four US frontier labs got gated in a single release cycle. Per the AI Central landscape review, here is the timeline worth keeping:

  • Fable 5 launched June 9, got suspended by Commerce on June 12 after an Amazon jailbreak produced exploit code, went dark worldwide for 19 days, and came back July 1 behind a 99% classifier and 50% usage caps.
  • Mythos 5 is now restricted to US organizations under a program called Project Glasswing, and GPT-5.6 previewed in tiers named Sol, Terra, and Luna to about 20 government-vetted orgs, with Sol running on Cerebras wafer-scale silicon at up to 750 tokens a second.
  • The reader takeaway is the boring one that pays off. Per Kris Torrington, any workflow that is revenue-critical or hard to recover by hand needs a fallback across a second vendor, a second jurisdiction, and a second deployment mode, because policy can pull your model access in an afternoon.

5. While the giants fought over permission, the agent tooling quietly got better. The builder layer had a strong day, and it is worth more than the drama. Per the Hermes list and Alex Finn, the Nous Hermes agent shipped a Mixture of Agents mode that aggregates answers through Opus 4.8 while pulling references from ChatGPT and DeepSeek, added a URL-to-skill command, and now handles full pull-request coding on the desktop, with Fable 5 slotted in as a pricey "Oracle" you call for hard builds rather than a daily driver.

  • Per YanXbt, a documented eight-agent setup covering roles from research to sales ops runs on a $7-a-month server and routes cheap work to DeepSeek and Gemini Flash and quality work to GPT-5.5 and Sonnet, landing at $29 to $93 a month against roughly $3,500 for one junior hire.
  • Per Dylan Davis, the real edge is not the model but the context you feed it, built through a flywheel of recording every meeting, logging why deals were won or lost, and codifying corrections into a home-base folder for a desktop agent.
  • One flare to watch, and to treat as a claim not a fact. Per the All-In Podcast, one host asserts Nvidia is sitting on a suppressed open-source model as good as Claude for most queries and is preparing to bundle it free to own the whole stack, a big if-true that carries no confirmation yet.

item.title: DeepSeek's big drop is a speed patch, not a smarter model, and the more interesting AI story this morning is who quietly owns the agent stack
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: DSpark turns out to be a serving-layer trick on the same V4 weights, the Nous Hermes tooling is maturing into a real platform, and a claim is circulating that Washington briefly cut Fable 5 and Mythos 5 off for everyone. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
DSpark turns out to be a serving-layer trick on the same V4 weights, the Nous Hermes tooling is maturing into a real platform, and a claim is circulating that Washington briefly cut Fable 5 and Mythos 5 off for everyone. Here is the signal.

The headline names today are DeepSeek and a policy scare, but the durable story is underneath. The value in AI keeps sliding from raw model capability toward the plumbing that runs agents cheaply and the judgment to point them at the right work. Here is what actually changed.

1. DeepSeek DSpark is a speed patch, and the number that matters is not the one being quoted. Read the model card before the hype. Per Kris Torrington, DSpark is not a new brain, it is the same DeepSeek V4 checkpoint with a speculative decoding module bolted on (a small draft model guesses the next tokens and the big model verifies them), so this is a serving upgrade, not a capability jump.

  • Reported speedups: V4 Flash runs about 60 to 85% faster per user, V4 Pro about 57 to 78% faster.
  • The specs underneath: V4 Flash is 284B parameters with 13B active, V4 Pro is 1.6 trillion with 49B active, both at a million-token context.
  • The catch: faster tokens do not mean your bill drops 85%. The only metric that pays you back is cost per completed workflow, and Torrington's own bar for switching is a 20 to 30% improvement on that, not a 5% one.

2. The Nous Hermes stack is turning into a real agent platform while nobody was looking. The open-agent side keeps shipping, and it is past toy stage. Per the Hermes list, two pieces landed that matter more than another benchmark tweet.

  • A Hermes browser extension hit v2: full session management, vision and screenshots, a side panel on every tab, and the ability to swap models mid-chat between Opus, Grok, GPT, and a local model. Eight-plus releases in a week.
  • A walkthrough of the Hermes Agent architecture showed the guts: 70-plus tools across roughly 28 toolsets, 18-plus providers, a 90-turn iteration budget, subagents capped at 50, and preflight context compression that kicks in at 50% full. It reads context files in priority order, checking its own `.hermes.md` before `AGENTS.md` before `CLAUDE.md`.

3. The local-model crowd is not a hobby anymore, it is a staffing model. The most interesting shift in the list is cultural. Per the Hermes list, Alex Finn described running four models across six computers 24/7 doing security scans, code optimization, scraping, and agent monitoring, and framed always-on local compute as unlimited Opus-level intelligence you do not meter. He still reaches for Fable 5 and Codex on the hard stuff, and that admission is the honest part. The takeaway from yesterday holds and sharpens: route the cheap and endless work to local swarms, and save the expensive frontier calls for the problems only you could have imagined.

4. Read the bayes near-future thread for the non-obvious bets, not the "AI will accelerate" part. A long forecast thread made the rounds, and most of it is the usual takeoff talk. Per bayes, the parts worth your attention are the specific ones.

  • The central wager: 4 to 7 more orders of magnitude of intelligence output per unit of scale are still on the table, which would make the current flat-looking frontier curve very misleading.
  • A compute-allocation squeeze that hits open source hardest: are your GB300s more valuable serving GLM 5.2 or building the next model inside a frontier lab? Capital does not fix it if the chips go to the frontier first.
  • A robotics ChatGPT-moment followed by an Opus-grade moment, with the gap between them measured in months, and physical scale-up toward roughly 100 million robots a year by 2030.

5. A policy scare to keep at arm's length, because the sourcing is thin. Handle this one carefully. Per Kris Torrington, the US government told Anthropic to restrict Fable 5 and Mythos 5 from foreign nationals, and because nationality could not be verified in real time, the models briefly went dark for everyone before access was restored. It advances yesterday's thread about Washington possibly pulling access to a major model, and it fits the new prediction markets on exactly that question. The honest note: this comes from a single creator with no named official, agency, or confirmation attached, so treat it as a claim to watch rather than an established fact.

The point that survives either way is the real one. If your whole stack sits on one frontier API, it can be working in the morning and cut off by policy that afternoon, and that is a dependency risk you can actually plan around. On a related thread, per the Market list, one investor is now betting against the standalone cybersecurity vendors on the theory that coding models are quietly eating their moat.

item.title: Fable 5 came back from an 18-day ban as the best model on the board, wrapped in guardrails that started a revolt, and it goes paid on Monday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.
item.content:encoded:
The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.

One model dominated every edition this week. Claude Fable 5 came back online after an 18-day export ban, and its return set off the whole chain of stories that followed: a capability leap the practitioners are calling real, a safety-gating backlash from the people who use it hardest, a pricing change that closes the free window on July 7, and a durable lesson about how to build so that no single model outage or price hike can hurt you again. If you only internalize one AI story from the week, this is it.

The arc, start to finish. The timeline matters, because the drama was in the sequence.

  • The ban and the return. Per AI Central, Fable 5 shipped June 9, was hit with a US export ban June 12, and came back to global availability July 1 after 18 days dark. The following day US Commerce formally lifted the export controls, which reframed the model as something closer to a national-security asset than a product launch. It returned wired with new cybersecurity classifiers and a routing change where routine coding requests can silently fall back to Opus 4.8.
  • The revolt. The gating is what the community actually reacted to. On the Hermes list, one heavy user called it "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reported it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers meant to route sensitive tasks to Opus were catching ordinary requests. The most useful reframe came from Alex Finn, who argued Fable 5 is better understood as an operating-system-level tool than a chat model, so you build around it rather than talk to it.
  • The capabilities, once you get past the guardrails. This is the part that justifies the noise. The Limitless podcast detailed what the model does when you clear the classifiers: it now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game with physics, sound, and spatial awareness from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is producing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.
  • The practitioner verdict. The people who ship agents for a living are the most convincing witnesses. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi called Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it, and the concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent posting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The economics, and why Monday matters. The free ride is short. Per Nate Herk, Fable 5 is priced at $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output, which is 2x Opus, the promotional period ends July 7, and on Claude plans you can only spend up to 50% of your weekly limits on Fable before it flips to usage credits. His honest read is that you realistically need Fable 5 for only 5% to 15% of your work, the genuinely hard long-horizon tasks, so the move this weekend is not to run it on everything but to point it at the few problems that actually need it. The Chase AI cost breakdown adds the lever that matters: the model's price swings enormously with its effort setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task, so on a paid meter the effort dial is your main cost control.

The lesson that outlasts the model. The most valuable takeaway is not about Fable 5 at all. It is the thing the 18-day ban taught. Per Nate B Jones, the firms that shrugged off the outage were the ones that own their harness, the scaffolding and tooling around a model rather than its raw intelligence, and simply rerouted to another model when Fable went dark. That is the discipline this whole episode should install: keep your harness model-agnostic so the next ban, price hike, or release is a config change instead of a migration.

The corollary Jones drew on the jobs side is worth holding too. The model does not run itself. The only roles it actually eliminates are the pure-execution ones with zero judgment. Everything else still needs a person to aim it, feed it context, check its work, and own the result, a role he calls the model manager. And as the model gets more capable, that aiming becomes the skill. The best takeaway of the week came from Dylan Davis: with a model this eager, your old maximalist prompts now backfire, and the new skill is restraint, telling it the destination and what done looks like, then getting out of its way.

What to watch next. Three things. First, July 7, when the free window closes and behavior changes, because a model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo, and the effort dial becomes the whole game. Second, GPT-5.6, which the market priced to land as soon as this week, arriving directly into the Fable comparison, though beware that early benchmarks were run against a Mythos preview and different harnesses, so the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Third, whether Anthropic loosens the classifiers, because right now the biggest thing standing between Fable 5 and its own capabilities is not a competitor, it is its own guardrails. Build model-agnostic, spend the effort dial deliberately, and use the free days you have left on the hard problems.

item.title: The AI story this weekend was not Fable 5 coming back, it was a Chinese open model good enough to make Gavin Baker rethink his whole framework
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.
item.content:encoded:
GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.

Fable 5 returning to subscriptions grabbed the headlines, but the durable story sits underneath it. A Chinese open-weight model reached the frontier, an experiment showed cheap models now match expensive ones on everyday work, and together they move the value in AI away from who executes and toward who knows what to ask. Here is what actually changed today.

1. GLM 5.2 is the model of the week, and it is not American. Per Jordi Visser, GLM 5.2 is the first Chinese open-weight model to credibly match or beat the US frontier, enough that Gavin Baker said it forced him to rethink his framework and one Databricks voice called it the Claude moment. Visser's nuance is the part to hold: the frontier still captures about 90% of the economic value even though open-source models process north of 80% of the tokens, so this is a margin threat, not a takeover, and it puts real pressure on the story that Anthropic grows revenue tenfold from here.

It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Meituan fully open-sourced LongCat-2.0 under an MIT license, a 1.6 trillion parameter mixture-of-experts model with a million-token context running on domestic chips. And per the Polymarket list, Alibaba banned its own employees from using Claude Code, while two fresh markets opened on whether the US removes public access to a major AI model, including a Chinese one, this year. The China-versus-US model axis is now the main axis.

2. Cheap models tie the expensive ones, so the value moved to imagination. The sharpest framing of the day. Per Nate B Jones, Mitchell Hashimoto ran ordinary feature work through a budget model for under a dollar in minutes, GPT-5.5 for about $1.50, and Fable 5 for about $9 and 40 minutes, all landing at the same quality, and everyone drew the obvious lesson to route cheap. The overlooked second test is the real one: Hashimoto handed the frontier model a gnarly systems optimization he designed himself, and it spent two hours and $40 reaching performance he says he could not have hit alone. The point is that no manager assigned that task, because it did not exist until an expert imagined it.

Execution is commoditizing, so the moat becomes the length of your list of things you know how to ask for. His leadership test is the quotable one: who on your team is allowed to pose a $400 question to a model today without asking permission? If the answer is nobody, that is an imagination limit, not a budget one.

3. If you keep Fable 5, stop giving it tasks and start giving it responsibilities. With Fable back on subscriptions only through 11:59pm Pacific on July 7, per the Hermes list, and then credits-only, how you drive it matters. Per Daria Cupareanu, the core shift, attributed to Anthropic's Claude Code lead Felix Rieseberg, is to give the model an outcome rather than a set of steps, because over-constraining boxes in the judgment that makes it good. The most concrete moves: tell it why the work matters and who it is for, do not default the effort dial to Max because High clears almost everything while Max burns quota for little gain, and make it verify its own output, which Anthropic's own team named the single highest-impact habit. One number worth filing: Fable runs at five times Sonnet 5's rate and twice Opus 4.8, and Anthropic admits it burns a weekly limit faster than any other model.

4. The tooling layer is quietly standardizing around agents. Two signals that the plumbing is maturing. Per Kun Chen, a principal engineer walked through a reproducible setup where the whole machine is declared in Nix so an agent that breaks something can be rebuilt in one script, all agents share a single global rules file symlinked into Claude, Codex, and the rest, and, most notably, he abandoned years of tmux for Herder, a terminal multiplexer built for the agent era that shows per-agent status natively and runs on Windows. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent side keeps shipping too, with the Nous Hermes agent exposing 47 slash commands and a growing skill-pack ecosystem, and Pliny releasing T3MP3ST, a multi-agent security swarm that turns a Claude Code or Codex subscription into an autonomous bug hunter claiming 90.1% on a benchmark where the incumbent scored 85%. That last one is a real dual-use flare to keep an eye on.

5. A new way to charge for AI, and a caution about swapping vendors. Two smaller shifts. Per Rick Orford, Cloudflare is building a toll booth for AI crawlers using the HTTP 402 payment-required response, with publishers now sending more than a billion payment notices a day and backers including the Associated Press, Reddit, and Pinterest, though Amazon rolled out a competing tool in June that is the real contest. And on the architecture-risk thread from this morning, per Kris Torrington, the warning is that a model can silently degrade while the dashboard stays green, and that is policy risk rather than model risk, with his line landing hard: if you move your whole stack from one dependency to another, you did not solve the problem, you just changed the logo on the risk.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the memory price hikes, the neocloud selloff, TSMC repricing 2nm wafers, and the Cloudflare investable ticker $NET all went there. Noted here: per the People-to-follow list, Grok 4.5 is in private beta at Tesla and SpaceX on a 1.5 trillion parameter model, with Musk claiming it is close to or beyond Opus and xAI promising a new foundation model monthly for the rest of 2026. Dropped: a thin get-rich listicle and a one-minute clip on government-owned AI, though both echo the same quiet theme, that running local and open models is becoming the hedge against centralized-AI policy risk.

item.title: The Fable 5 ban taught the real lesson this weekend: one vendor in one country is one point of failure
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.
item.content:encoded:
A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.

The weekend's best AI writing was not about a new model. It was about what the Fable 5 outage actually proved, and the answer is an architecture warning every builder should act on. Underneath it, the demand data went vertical, the open-source camp shipped, and the practitioners quietly agreed on how to run these models. Here is the shift.

1. The real lesson of the ban is single vendor, single jurisdiction, single failure point. The cleanest post-mortem of the shutdown, a breakdown of whether the US could ban open-source AI, walks the timeline: Anthropic released Fable 5 and its lighter-guardrail twin Mythos 5 on June 9, an Amazon researcher found a narrow way to bypass Fable's safeguards on vulnerability prompts, and on June 12 the US applied export controls ordering Anthropic to restrict foreign-national access. Because nationality could not be verified in real time, both models went dark for everyone.

The key clarification is what did not happen: no open-weight model was pulled from Hugging Face, no repo was wiped. Frontier AI is now treated like advanced chips, where the lever is access, not quality. The takeaway is a rule, not a vibe: any revenue-critical or customer-facing workflow needs a fallback across vendor, jurisdiction, and deployment mode, because the failure can be silent, your dashboard green while the model underneath is quietly weaker or gated.

2. The route-around went straight to Chinese models. The same breakdown notes that when Fable went dark, builders did not wait. Some dropped to cheaper models, some went local, and a real share started testing the Chinese open models GLM 5.2 and Kimi, which are lower cost with an open door, accepting the different jurisdiction and trust profile as the price of continuity. That is the uncomfortable second-order effect of an export control aimed at denying access: it hands the open Chinese labs a live audition with exactly the builders the control was meant to keep on domestic frontier models.

3. The demand data went vertical, and that is why the labs can afford all this. Per the Crypto list, OpenRouter token volume ran from 6.4T in the first week of January to 14.8T weekly by March, up 160% in two months, and H100 rental prices, after falling for two years, reversed and climbed 40% in five months to $2.35. Enterprise AI adoption is still under 30% by the same desk's estimate, and that is the bull and the warning at once: the usage curve is near vertical and most of the enterprise has not even started. The tokens are real, and that is the ground truth under every capex headline.

4. Nous Research is turning Hermes into an operating layer. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent camp shipped all weekend. Co-founder Teknium announced an expanded plugin interface so developers with long-pending pull requests can ship stable plugins without merge dependency, Tony Simons pushed the Hermes A2A Bridge to v0.4.8 with local agent-to-agent interop, and DODOREACH wired a robodog as the first physical Hermes pet with spontaneous text-to-speech. Kevin Simback's Hermes Atlas newsletter frames the thesis plainly, that Hermes is becoming an operating layer for AI work positioned for the sovereign and open-source movement. This is the counterweight to section one: the answer to single-vendor risk is partly the open stack maturing into something you can actually run yourself.

5. The pros have settled on a division of labor, and it is Fable plans, Codex executes. Per the Hermes list, Matthew Berman is paying $200 a month each for two services and splitting the work, Fable for planning and GPT-5.5 for execution. Theo from t3.gg posted his own config, Fable plans and Codex executes, calling Codex far better at computer use, UI verification, and executing well-specified work, and noting he was throwing away about half his agent-driven pull requests before the workflow and now closes almost none. The lesson from yesterday's restraint argument lands here as a concrete setup: use the strongest model to decide what done looks like, then hand a tightly-scoped build to the executor.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the $700B hyperscaler capex and the neocloud selloff, as the trade. Noted here but light on specifics: Polymarket prices a 62% chance the next Gemini Pro model ships July 17 to match Anthropic's Mythos, and per the Crypto list Anthropic's Mythos tool is being used by Coinbase and Binance to stress-test infrastructure even as a Vercel-adjacent breach exposed crypto-dev API keys, a reminder the agent security surface is widening. Dropped: the Nate B Jones "Fable 5 wants the whole job" clip returned only a fragment with no new specifics beyond yesterday's restraint theme.

item.title: The whole skill just flipped from pushing the model to reining it in, right as the free tokens turn into a trap
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.
item.content:encoded:
Dylan Davis says your best old prompts now make Fable 5 worse, Nate B Jones shows how to burn the free weekend tokens on hard problems, and the All-In crew warns the giveaway is a trojan horse for the app layer.

For two years the AI skill was the same: push harder, prompt longer, add steps until it did enough. This weekend that skill inverted. The new models do too much, the labs are telling you to get out of the way, and the free tokens everyone is stacking come with a catch. Here is the shift, the playbook, and the warning.

1. Your best prompt is now a liability, and the fix is restraint. Dylan Davis, who runs an AI consultancy, argues the new generation makes your old prompts backfire. Fable 5 will run for hours, take actions nobody asked for, even claim work it never did. His fix is three controls plus one caveat.

  • Cut the scaffolding. Straight from Anthropic's own Fable 5 user guide: old prompts are too prescriptive and degrade output on smarter models. Anthropic cut 80% of the prompt behind Claude Code for the new models. They call it un-hobbling, getting out of the way.
  • Prompt the destination, not the steps. The template is a goal, the audience, and most of all what done looks like in binary terms. Point it and say go, because without a finish line it over-engineers.
  • The fence. Split thinking from acting. Tell it plainly to assess and then stop and do nothing, then in the action phase tell it to do the simplest thing that gets the one task done and nothing else.
  • The dial. Reasoning runs low, medium, high, extra, max. Reserve extra and max for genuinely hard multi-hour work, because those levels spawn parallel sub-agents and the cost climbs fast.
  • Cherish the pushback. When Fable 5 disagrees with your feedback, do not override it. Ask what it knows that you do not, and what would change its mind.
  • Two catches. Fable 5 goes paid after roughly July 7 regardless of your plan, so save it for the hardest tasks. And in this early government-gated rollout it may silently downgrade you to Opus 4.8 on biology or cybersecurity questions and stay dumb for the whole chat, so start a fresh conversation to get the good model back.

2. If the tokens are free this weekend, aim them at something hard. Nate B Jones's free-token playbook is the practical companion to the restraint argument. His moves: build a goal harness to steer Codex 5.5 toward an outcome rather than micromanaging it, plug the model into real tools like Blender so it acts instead of just talks, keep prompts short, and point all of it at a hard business problem rather than toy demos while the compute is subsidized. Same lesson as Davis from the other direction: set the destination, then let the model work.

3. But the free tokens are a trojan horse, and the target is the app layer. The All-In crew's warning is the strategic frame under the giveaway. Free tokens are not generosity, they are customer acquisition, and the analogy they reach for is Microsoft using cheap Excel and Word to bury WordPerfect and Lotus. The models are commoditizing, so the real prize is the application layer on top, and OpenAI is spending to own it before anyone else does. Take the free compute, but know what it is buying them.

4. The margin is moving to the orchestration layer. This is the tooling half of a thesis that also runs in Investing today. Milk Road's AI desk argues the money moves past the frontier model to whoever orchestrates the work: route the cheap, high-volume tasks to small or local models and reserve the frontier model for the hard calls. Coinbase is the cited example of a shop wiring exactly that kind of cost-aware routing. Dylan Patel's claim that Opus 4.8 API tokens run near 80% margins is why the labs can afford to give the entry tokens away and still win.

5. The agents shipping this week are doing real, pointed jobs. From the Hermes ecosystem roundup, the pattern is narrow agents that undercut incumbents:

  • UNBROKER, an agent that hunts down your personal data across data-broker sites and files the removals, aimed squarely at undercutting subscription services like DeleteMe.
  • Grok Composer 2.5 reportedly cloned the Rampage app in under 120 seconds, another data point that the build-an-app moat is thinning fast.
  • Hermes Vault v0.18.0 shipped, continuing the open agentic-tooling drumbeat from the Nous Research corner.

Filtered. Vox Day's Winter Market post was an AI-music-video devlog with no named model, tool, or technique worth passing on, so it was dropped. The equity side of the orchestration and capex debate, including the neocloud and hyperscaler names, went to Investing. The immigration and creator-economy segments from the same All-In and Galloway sources went to News.

item.title: Fable 5 is the best model on the board and it may leave in days, so the useful question is how to use it cheaply right now
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.
item.content:encoded:
The practitioners who actually ship agents say Fable 5 is a step change, its own team is publishing the playbook for cutting the cost 80% before it goes, and the China price war just turned into a distillation fight.

The thread running through this morning is urgency. Fable 5 is drawing the strongest praise yet from the people who build agents for a living, and by their own account it is priced to leave via the API in a matter of days. So the practical move is not to debate whether it is good but to learn the cost playbook while you still can. Under that sits a China story that stopped being about price and became about who copied whom.

1. The people who ship agents for a living say Fable 5 is a genuine step change. This is not lab marketing, it is the practitioner tape. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi are calling Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, and Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it. The concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent reporting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The catch is the clock. Per Chase AI, the team behind it has signaled Fable 5 is "leaving via API pricing in a few days," so the window to build against it at current terms is short. If you have a real agent workload, this is the weekend to run it.

2. The cost playbook matters more than the benchmark, and the team published it. A frontier model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo. The useful Chase AI breakdown is that Fable 5's price swings enormously with its "effort" setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task.

  • At max effort it runs about $22 a task at a 70% pass rate. That is real money once a background agent is looping on its own.
  • Dropped to low effort it falls to about $3.76 a task, more than 80% cheaper, and still beats Opus 4.8 on the same work.
  • The orchestration trick is to use the expensive setting as a planner or advisor that hands specs to cheaper models, rather than paying top rate for every keystroke. A "Ponytail" configuration reportedly trims another 20%.

The takeaway is that the cost of intelligence is now a tuning problem, not a fixed price. Most teams are overpaying because they run one setting for everything.

3. The killer use case is cloning software you already pay for. The demo that lands in Chase AI's use-case rundown is pointing the model's deep-research mode at an existing tool and having it rebuild a working version. The worked example pairs it with WhisperFlow, the dictation app, to reconstruct the feature set from scratch. The same loop covers auditing your own usage, standing up an agentic operating layer, and running code review. Treat these as proof the build-versus-buy line is moving, not as turnkey products yet.

4. Hermes shipped an agent release built entirely around not lying to you. Nous Research pushed Hermes Agent v0.18.0, the "Judgement Release," per the Hermes list. The design is a direct answer to the reliability complaint that has dogged autonomous agents. It adds completion contracts (the agent must satisfy an explicit checklist before it declares a task done), an evidence ledger that records why it believes each step succeeded, and a "goal_judge" that grades its own output. The interesting shift is that the frontier of agent work is moving from raw capability to verifiable honesty about what the agent actually did.

5. The China AI fight stopped being about price and became about distillation. For months the story was Chinese labs undercutting the frontier on cost. This week it inverted. Per the Financial Markets list, DeepSeek is raising its V4 Pro price to about 12 yuan (roughly $1.77) per million output tokens (the unit models are billed in), up from 6 yuan after a 75% cut in May, which reads as a company that thinks it is winning share and no longer needs to buy it.

At the same time Alibaba reportedly banned Anthropic's Claude Code over a "distillation scandal," distillation being the practice of training a cheaper model on a more expensive one's outputs. The accusation cuts both directions in this market, and it is now the sharp edge of the US-China model race. Separately, on the All-In show notes, Palantir and Nvidia are reported to be pairing on open Nemotron models, with Karp pushing government customers toward open weights. That is the same open-versus-closed argument playing out on the US side.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Bittensor and its TAO token, the decentralized-AI-compute network from Milk Road, is a real AI-infrastructure story but trades as a token, so it went to Investing as a satellite position. The Fable 5 API-pricing exit is also a stock-adjacent event, but the actionable read here is the build window, so the market angle stays in Investing. The GPT-5.6 launch timing and the Nasdaq "next Claude Sonnet release" prediction market are noise until something ships, so they wait.

item.title: Fable 5 is back for real, and the day's other news is that the labs suddenly make money
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.
item.content:encoded:
The model everyone missed returned with new guardrails and genuinely new tricks, Anthropic reportedly turned its first profit while a startup chip threatens Nvidia on inference, and the cheap-clone war got a price cut aimed straight at China.

This morning the story was that Fable 5 came back so locked down its power users revolted. This afternoon fills in the other half. The model can actually do things again, the economics under the whole industry quietly turned positive, and the frontier labs are now pricing to kill their Chinese clones. The unit-economics doubt from this morning got a real answer.

1. Fable 5 is genuinely back, and the capabilities are the part worth your attention. The guardrail revolt from this morning is real, but the Limitless podcast fills in what the model does when you get past them. It now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the websites and artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game (physics, sound, spatial awareness) from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is doing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.

The new safeguards are the price of the return. Fable now monitors every prompt for attack patterns and routes anything it flags to a heavier, slower model, and that is why ordinary requests keep getting caught. The practical facts to plan around, from The AI Advantage: it burns roughly 70% more tokens than Opus 4.8 (not the 2x people assumed), it is free only through July 7 before usage-based pricing kicks in, and it carries its own 50% weekly usage cap. If your work has real judgment in it, this is the sharpest tool on the shelf right now. Just budget for it.

2. The unit-economics story flipped today, and that answers this morning's bear case. The 5:30am edition ran OpenAI's cash burn as evidence the whole thing leaks money. The afternoon numbers cut the other way.

  • Anthropic reportedly turned profitable in Q2 2026, the first frontier lab to do it, on inference margins said to run near 80%. If that holds, the "nobody makes money on inference" thesis is already dated.
  • OpenAI claims it halved its own inference cost, dropping the GPU count needed to serve free-tier users from hundreds of thousands to a couple hundred, per the same podcast. Take the exact figures with salt, but the direction matches Anthropic's.
  • Etched is the wild card in the hardware stack. The inference-chip startup has raised $800M and booked $1B in orders for a chip that has not shipped, promising 75% less power for the same inference output versus a GPU. The backers are the story: Peter Thiel, Jane Street, and TSMC, which took equity. A chip foundry taking a stake in a would-be Nvidia alternative is the kind of signal that does not show up in a benchmark. It ships by year end.

Put together, the picture is not an industry bleeding out. It is one where the cost of serving a model is falling fast enough that the margins are finally showing up.

3. Sonnet 5 got priced to kill the cheap-clone trade, and the distillation war went public. The strategic move today was pricing. Sonnet 5 launched cheaper than Sonnet 4.6 (through August 31) while doing roughly Opus 4.8's job, an open bid to keep builders inside the Claude ecosystem instead of drifting to a subsidized Chinese model like GLM 5.2. This is the same cost-curve pressure from this morning, answered with a price cut instead of a shrug.

The uglier subplot: that "spyware-like" code people found in Claude Code was reportedly an experiment to detect distillation attacks (rivals training their cheap models on the outputs of an expensive one). Alibaba's Qwen got caught doing exactly that, and Dario Amodei confirmed it publicly. DeepSeek V4 Flash still sits at the top of the open-model rankings, so the cheap tier keeps closing the gap. The frontier's answer is to make its own mid-tier too cheap to bother cloning.

4. If you are actually building with this stuff, here is the day's practical playbook. The most useful content today was not the model news, it was people showing how to run these tools without going broke or hitting a wall.

  • Cut Fable's cost by dialing effort, not switching models. Per Chase AI, the same task ran $22 at max effort and $3.76 at low, an 80%-plus cut, and Fable-on-low still beat Opus-on-max. Token-saver skills like ponytail (about 22% cheaper on Fable) and caveman help, and `/deep-research` can fan out over a hundred sub-agents when you actually need the depth.
  • Every agent demo dies at the last step, so design for it. Nate B Jones laid out a reusable agent skeleton of nine primitives (context pack, ingest, chunk, normalize, store, retrieve, cite, export, and a human gate at the end) and pointed it at real drudgery like insurance appeals and tax prep. His point ties to the cost story: once your data is clean, a cheap or open model does the job, so the moat is the plumbing, not the model.
  • Build a second brain that any model can read. Nate Herk wired Andrej Karpathy's LLM-wiki idea into Claude Code and Obsidian: drop in sources, the model splits them into cross-linked markdown pages with routing rules, and any agent can reason over the result. Fable understood a "keep it simple for a beginner" design instruction that Opus 4.8 could not get after a full day. Because it is just markdown with routing, it is model-agnostic.
  • UNBROKER, an open-source privacy tool for your weekend. Released as a Hermes skill by the Nous Research crowd, it finds where data brokers expose your personal info and files the legal removals for you, all locally. It is a free replacement for the $330-a-year DeleteMe tier, and a clean example of an agent pointed at a boring, valuable job.

5. One caveat before you trust next week's benchmark war. GPT-5.6 is priced to land as soon as Tuesday, and the wiki-building demo surfaced a sharp catch: OpenAI benchmarked GPT-5.6 Sol against a Mythos preview model, not against Fable 5, and used different test harnesses. So when the launch numbers hit, the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Read the release, but do not let the marketing pick your model. The move that got everyone through the Fable outage still holds: keep your harness model-agnostic so next week's drop is a config change.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. The Meta compute-glut refutation (Alexandr Wang's Watermelon model), the Nvidia revenue-share model, and the Anthropic-versus-Micron capex fight are the market story in Investing. The Kris Torrington Fable recap and the "Fable is a magician" clip repeated what section one already covers, so they were folded, not listed twice. The self-help anxiety video and a culture-war newsletter were out of scope. Alibaba banning its own staff from using Claude Code went to News with the rest of the US-China AI friction.

item.title: Fable 5 came back guardrailed to the point of revolt, and the price of intelligence is falling
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.
item.content:encoded:
The relaunched model is so locked down that power users say it is unusable for real work, China keeps undercutting the frontier on cost, and the useful question is what actually still needs a human.

The model everyone wanted back is back, and the first thing its heaviest users did was complain that they cannot use it. That gap, between the model on the benchmark and the model you are allowed to run, is the real story this morning.

1. Fable 5 returned so locked down that power users are in revolt. The export controls came off yesterday. The guardrails did not. On the Hermes list, the reaction is loud and specific: one heavy user says Fable 5 is "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reports it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers that route sensitive tasks to Opus are catching ordinary requests. The counter-take, from Alex Finn, is that Fable 5 is better understood as an operating-system-level tool than a chat model, so you build around it rather than talk to it.

Sonnet 5, meanwhile, is getting called "absurdly expensive" for what it does. The through-line with yesterday's fingerprint fight is the same: the frontier model you read about and the one you can actually deploy are drifting apart, and for real work the guardrails are now a bigger constraint than the intelligence.

2. The price of intelligence is falling, and China is why. This is the model-economics side of a story whose market side sits in Investing. The framing from Capital Flows that matters for builders is that a subsidized Chinese lab does not need to earn a margin on inference at all, so it can give away a model one generation back and still fund the next one. The export controls proved they cannot be contained: weights that already exist everywhere cannot be recalled, and the June suspension of the US frontier model was answered by an open-source near-peer within a day.

DeepSeek V4 Flash now sits at the top of the Hermes model rankings. If you are choosing a model to build on, the cheap-and-open tier keeps getting closer to the frontier while the frontier gets harder to actually use.

3. The honest read on Fable 5 and jobs: it kills the work with no judgment in it. Nate B Jones had the cleanest labor take, and it cuts against the panic. The only jobs a model this capable actually eliminates are the pure-execution roles with zero judgment, the ones that could honestly have been automated any time in the last decade. Everything else still needs a person, because the model does not run itself. Someone has to aim it, feed it the right context, check what it produced, and own the result when it lands on a desk. He calls that role the model manager, and it is a different job than the one you have now, not a smaller one. If your work carries real judgment, you are the one pointing the tool, not the one it replaces.

4. The release calendar is about to get loud. Two frontier drops are priced as near-certain. The market puts GPT-5.6 landing as soon as Tuesday at 77% and Grok 4.4 by month-end at 78%. That matters for anyone building right now, because a GPT-5.6 that lands into the current AI-bubble argument (the overbuild and cost-curve doubts in Investing) becomes a referendum on whether frontier gains still justify frontier spend.
